summary: The notebook provides a complete inference pipeline for the Vesuvius Challenge Surface Detection task, leveraging a Keras-JAX backend and the medicai library. It loads pre-trained 3D segmentation models (TransUNet and SegFormer), applies test-time augmentation through spatial flips and axial rotations, and processes large volumes using sliding window inference. Predictions are refined with a custom 3D post-processing routine that combines hysteresis thresholding, anisotropic morphological closing, and dust removal before packaging results into a submission ZIP. Readers should learn how to efficiently structure 3D inference, apply TTA for robustness, and use domain-specific morphological operations to clean segmentation outputs.
competition_flow: Loads test TIFF volumes, normalizes intensities, runs sliding window inference with TTA on two pre-trained 3D models, applies 3D morphological post-processing, and exports the final masks as a submission ZIP.
data_reading: Reads test.csv for image IDs using pandas.
Loads 3D TIFF files with tifffile.imread, converts to np.float32, and expands dimensions to (1, D, H, W, 1).
ensembling: A weighted ensemble averages the logits from both models using weights [0.8, 0.2], applies softmax, and takes the argmax to generate final predictions.
insights: Sliding window inference with Gaussian overlap enables processing large 3D volumes within limited VRAM.
Test-time augmentation via spatial flips and axial rotations improves prediction stability without additional training.
3D hysteresis thresholding combined with anisotropic morphological closing effectively connects fragmented surface predictions and removes noise.
